1999-07-01.title: Russian Bombers Face Off Against American Fighters
1999-07-01.abstract: National Security Council aide says Russian bombers flew close enough to Iceland and Norway last week, during Russia's largest military exercise since Soviet Union's breakup, to cause United States and Norway to scramble fighter planes as precaution; say Russians apparently did not violate Iceland's or Norway's air space in incidents reminiscent of cold war confrontations; sees incidents linked to recent tensions over Russia's role in Kosovo (M)

1999-07-01.title: U.N. Takes Up Plan to Limit Population
1999-07-01.abstract: United Nations General Assembly begins debating action plan first drawn up five years ago to limit world population growth, although developing countries remain deeply divided over some aspects of plan; Sec Gen Kofi Annan cites importance of stabilizing population (S)

1999-07-01.title: Barak Creates Wide Coalition With 7 Parties
1999-07-01.abstract: Israeli Prime Min-elect Ehud Barak forges broad coalition Government, clinching last deal with ultra-Orthodox Shas Party; Government will consist of seven extremely different parties, mixing secular with religious, left with right and settlers with backers of Palestinian state; is widely expected to back Barak's plan to move forward on peace deals with Palestinians and Syria; Barak will be own Defense Minister; table (M)

1999-07-01.title: New Frontier for Mobile-Phone Operators Lies Underground
1999-07-01.abstract: PARIS—  If you flinch every time you hear an electronic approximation of Beethoven's "Fuer Elise" on a mobile telephone, you will be grateful that few mobile-phone operators have installed widespread networks in underground metro systems around the world.

1999-07-01.title: Turkey Reports No Strife After Kurd's Sentence
1999-07-01.abstract: No incidents are reported in Turkey in wake of death sentence given to Kurdish rebel leader, Abdullah Ocalan; police remain on alert; Kurds stage demonstrations in several European cities and in Australia, but none escalate into serious violence; several arson attacks are reported on Turkish businesses in Germany (M)

1999-07-02.title: Joshua Nkomo of Zimbabwe Is Dead at 62
1999-07-02.abstract: Joshua Nkomo, once-fiery African nationalist and guerrilla leader who was father to Zimbabwe's struggle for independence, dies at age 62; photo (M)

1999-07-02.title: It Is Bright, but Will Men Wear It?: Color Is Back as Men Rediscover Their 'Inner Peacock'
1999-07-02.abstract: LONDON—  After seasons of gray, gray and more gray, bright color is making a comeback. Menswear designers are showing a rainbow of colors on the runways of Europe for spring 2000. But the question remains: Will men buy it?

1999-07-02.title: Lott Says That, Despite Snags, Holbrooke Is Likely to Be Confirmed
1999-07-02.abstract: Senate majority leader Trent Lott, with at least three Senators blocking final vote to confirm Richard C Holbrooke as chief American diplomat at United Nations, says that nomination 'has some problems' but that Holbrooke is still likely to be confirmed; identity of only one of Senators blocking nomination is known publicly: Sen Charles E Grassley, Iowa Republican, who has said that he will prevent vote on Holbrooke's nomination until State Dept resolves unrelated dispute with employee described as whistle-blower (S)

1999-07-02.title: Gucci's Glam Slam vs. Prada's Vanity Sportswear
1999-07-02.abstract: MILAN—  The Italian menswear season, which closes Friday, has been about romancing the male. The fashion appeal has two aspects: sex and sport. And there are twin team-leaders: Gucci and Prada.

1999-07-02.title: P.C. Exports Eased
1999-07-02.abstract: Technological advances that have made personal computers as powerful as supercomputers were just a few years ago prompted President Clinton to greatly ease restrictions on exports of high-speed machines to countries like Russia and China.      Article, page A4.

1999-07-03.title: Next Israeli Leader Steps Out of His Silence
1999-07-03.abstract: Prime Min-elect Ehud Barak of Israel, who will be sworn in on July 7, is emerging from behind scenes to take his place as country's leader; Barak telephones Yasir Arafat, Palestinian leader, for their first official conversation; two make date to meet 'soon' and to restart stagnant peace effort; Barak also reproaches Pres Clinton for his seemingly offhand remark about wanting Palestinian refugees to live wherever they want to live; ultimate disposition of Palestian refugees has been left for final status talks, which have not yet begun (M)

1999-07-03.title: World Briefing
1999-07-03.abstract: French appeals court rejects requests for further investigation into car crash in Paris that killed Princess Diana and Mohamed (Dodi) al-Fayed and their driver in 1997; investigating judges and prosecutors must now decide whether to bring charges in case (S)

1999-07-03.title: Robert Polhill, 65, Lebanon Hostage for 3 Years
1999-07-03.abstract: Robert B Polhill, American business professor who was held hostage for 39 months in Lebanon, dies at age 65; Polhill, former heavy smoker, learned he had throat cancer four days after he was freed in April 1990 (S)

1999-07-03.title: NATO BARS RUSSIA FROM REINFORCING TROOPS IN KOSOVO
1999-07-03.abstract: United States and its NATO allies are blocking Russia from flying hundreds of troops into Kosovo before details of Russia's role in peacekeeping force could be worked out; Russia has been denied use of airspace over Hungary, Romania and Bulgaria to fly planeloads of peacekeepers into Pristina, after those nations consulted with American and NATO officials; Hungary is new member of NATO, and Romania and Bulgaria seek to join it; Russian military negotiators are trying to alter agreement reached in Helsinki, by having their troops in Kosovo report only to Russian officers, rather than be under formal command of NATO; Russia also wants to station forces in Italian, French, American and German sectors of Kosovo; Clinton Administration officials say they want no more surprises from Russia, given its surprise deployment of 200 troops to Pristina airport last month (M)

1999-07-03.title: Lirong Journal; Tibetans, and Vultures, Keep Ancient Burial Rite
1999-07-03.abstract: Tibetans have three principal ways to return their dead to earth; in sky burial, body is dismembered and fed to vultures; in water burial, corpse is cut into small pieces and fed to fish; third way, cremation, is too expensive for poorest Tibetans; photo; map (M)

1999-07-04.title: Britain Awaits Rogue Trader With Interest In the Spoils
1999-07-04.abstract: Nicholas W Leeson, the Rogue Trader who brought down Barings merchant bank in 1995, is set to fly back to Britain after almost four years in a Singapore jail for fraud and forgery; his assets are reportedly frozen, his future is unclear and his freedom flawed by reports that he bragged in prison about having stashed away millions from the days when he traded fast and loose in Singapore; photo (M)

1999-07-04.title: Suffrage Issue Dominating a Kuwait Election
1999-07-04.abstract: Kuwaitis are voting in what may be country's last all-male elections, after a campaign dominated by heated national discussion of whether it is time to allow women political rights; many candidates for seats in Parliament vow to block decree by the Emir, Sheik Jaber al-Ahmed al-Sabah, that would allow Kuwaiti women to vote in next election (M)

1999-07-04.title: Roadblock to a Peace Pact: Irish Mostly Say 'No'
1999-07-04.abstract: Warren Hoge analysis of the deadlock that has halted progress in putting peace accord into place in Northern Ireland and the decision by Prime Ministers Tony Blair of Britain and Bertie Ahern of Ireland to issue their own solution to the impasse and demand that Ulster politicians accept it or lose their right to govern themselves (M)

1999-07-04.title: Church of Milosevic's Rise Now Sends Mixed Message
1999-07-04.abstract: Some Serbian Orthodox Church priests are demanding resignation of Slobodan Milosevic as Yugoslav President, but the church continues to give priests a mixed message on what they should do in toppling a President who is an indicted war criminal; church, which supported Milosevic's wars against Croatia and Bosnia, still embraces his nationalist aspirations, while rejecting the savage means he uses to achieve them; photo of Patriarch Pavle, who has called Milosevic's policies in Kosovo criminal (M)

1999-07-04.title: Kashmir Thwarts India-Pakistan Attempt at Trust
1999-07-04.abstract: Fragile efforts begun in February to build trust between India and Pakistan are thwarted by operation of Pakistani military aimed at taking craggy peaks in Indian-held Kashmir that overlook a crucial Indian supply line; sense of betrayal runs deep on Indian side; experts believe that the likeliest explanation is that the push for the Kargil operation came from Pakistan's military and that Prime Min Nawaz Sharif went along, enthusiastically or not, believing Pakistan could plausibly deny authorship (M)

1999-07-05.title: Stresses of Milosevic's Rule Blamed for Decline in Births
1999-07-05.abstract: Serbian women in unprecedented numbers are deciding not to have children, primiarly because of anxiety about the war in Kosovo, fear of civil war in Serbia and unwillingness to raise children in a nation whose standard of living has declined every year for the past decade; abortion rate appears to be rising sharply and increasing numbers of young women are telling doctors they do not want to give birth; photo; map (M)

1999-07-05.title: Key Foe, Back From Exile, Dares Milosevic to Jail Him
1999-07-05.abstract: Zoran Djindjic, Serbian opposition figure who fled country during war with NATO, returns and dares Pres Slobodan Milosevic to arrest him; faces up to 20 years in prison for avoiding military service during Kosovo war, as well as daunting political problems inside fractured opposition movement; photo (M)

1999-07-05.title: Trader Who Broke Barings Is Back in Britain From Asian Jail
1999-07-05.abstract: Nicholas W Leeson, arriving in Britain after four years in a Singapore jail, says he is sorry for the wild securities trading he engaged in that brought down Barings Bank; Barings liquidators allow him access to $7,900 a month of his money; Barings' creditors want to know about his financial gains from 1996 memoir (M)

1999-07-05.title: Syrian President Due in Russia Today as Mideast Diplomacy Starts to Revive
1999-07-05.abstract: Surprise trip to Russia by Pres Hafez al-Assad of Syria is seen as first step in an expected flurry of Middle East diplomacy as Arab and Israeli leaders jockey for position in peace effort that is suddenly showing signs of life for the first time in three years (M)

1999-07-05.title: U.S.  IS  EXPECTING KASHMIR PULLBACK BY PAKISTANI SIDE
1999-07-05.abstract: Pres Clinton and Prime Min Nawaz Sharif of Pakistan meet for three hours, after which Administration officials say they expect Pakistani-backed forced to soon withdraw from their mountain redoubt on Indians side of cease-fire line in Kashmir; officials are uncertain whether Sharif will be able to guarantee a withdrawal of forces because people of Pakistan and its military leaders are likely to resist backing down and because Sharif does not have full control over the mountain fighters or even his own military command, which has run the Government overtly or from the shadows for much of its history; photo (M)

1999-07-06.title: After Scotland Yard Inquiry, A Village in Kosovo Buries 64
1999-07-06.abstract: Bodies of 64 ethnic Albanians, slain by Serbs in village of Bela Crkva hours after NATO bombing began on March 25, are reburied following exhumation from mass graves for collection of evidence by war crimes investigators from Scotland Yard; emotional scene described; Scotland Yard's chief investigator John T Bunn says 60 victims were shot in individual groups, in deliberate fashion, along riverbank; photos (M)

1999-07-06.title: Paris Journal; An Old Prediction Speeds France's Annual Exodus
1999-07-06.abstract: Dire predictions of Nostradamus that apocalypse will descend across swath of northern Europe on August 11 speeds exodus of French residents on annual five-week vacations; many are heeding advice of well-known designer Paco Rabanne in best-selling book, 1999: Fire From the Heavens, to stay at least 60 kilometers from Paris; photo (M)

1999-07-06.title: Pakistani's Pullout Vow: A Very Hard Sell at Home
1999-07-06.abstract: News analysis: Pakistan's Prime Min Nawaz Sharif is likely to find it difficult to deliver on pledge to US following White House meeting with Pres Clinton to withdraw Pakistani-backed forces from Indian side of cease-fire line in disputed territory of Kashmir; few Pakistanis see Sharif's sudden decision to visit Clinton, and pledge to take concrete steps to restore cease-fire line, as breakthrough that could end limited, undeclared war with India; most see it either as sell-out of Kashmir cause or another ill-fated move in diplomatic game of chess; photo (M)

1999-07-06.title: Departing Foreign Aid Chief Says Cuts Are Dangerous
1999-07-06.abstract: Interview with J Brian Atwood, outgoing administrator of Agency for International Development, who issues unusually harsh assessment of Clinton Administration and Congress, faulting them for aid budget he says is dangerously inadequate; accuses Administration of allowing itself to be pushed around by Congress, and warns legislative cuts in foreign aid have created crisis for US; photo (M)

1999-07-06.title: World Briefing
1999-07-06.abstract: EUROPE    NORTHERN IRELAND: BLAIR CLARIFICATION -- Seeking to shore up wavering Protestant support for his blueprint for setting up the new Northern Ireland government this month, Prime Minister Tony Blair told the House of Commons that Sinn Fein would have to give up its cabinet positions if its I.R.A. allies did not disarm on schedule. Mr. Blair issued the clarification in a speech arguing that the plan, negotiated in Belfast last week, was the best opportunity for peace this generation of Britons would ever have.   Warren Hoge (NYT)

1999-07-07.title: VOWING TO PURSUE PEACE WITH ARABS, BARAK TAKES OVER
1999-07-07.abstract: Ehud Barak is sworn in as Prime Minister of Israel, and he pledges in his inaugural speech that pursuit of true, lasting peace will be his foremost concern; reaches out to all his domestic constituencies; speaks of pain and death on both sides of Israeli-Palestinian conflict and suggests that both sides stop focusing on settling of historical wrongs; says he will move forward on both peace tracks at once in effort to allay fears of Palestinians that negotiations with Syria will take precedence; repeats campaign promise to withdraw Israeli troops from southern Lebanon within a year; his words are received by Palestinians in Gaza as welcome break from perceived hostility of his predecessor, Benjamin Netanyahu, and Palestinian leaders express cautious optimism; photos (M)

1999-07-07.title: Old Greens In Germany Challenged By Upstarts
1999-07-07.abstract: Forty younger members of German Green Party issue manifesto scoring 'moralizing know-it-alls' from founding generation of 1960's; are demanding resignation of top leader, Jurgen Trittin, who is also German Environment Minister (M)

1999-07-07.title: India and U.N. Working On Kashmir Plane Rules
1999-07-07.abstract: United Nations and India struggle to resolve dispute over closing of airport in Indian city of Srinagar that India closed to international civilian traffic when fighting erupted in Kashmir in May; dispute ostensibly involves UN use of airport, which lies south of line that divides hostile Indian and Pakistani forces in Kashmir; also reflects far broader tensions between UN and India, which has refused since 1972 to recognize UN involvement in fate of disputed Himalayan territory; map (M)

1999-07-07.title: U.S., Avoiding Castro, Relaxes Rules on Cuba
1999-07-07.abstract: Clinton Administration moves quietly to expand contacts between US and Cuba, pressing modest opening that largely sidesteps Government of Pres Fidel Castro; is seeking mainly to ease hardships of Cuban people and allow certain Americans to visit Cuba, open offices there and send money, food and medicine more freely; will lift more restrictions on money transfers and travel and announce start of direct charter flights between Havana and New York and Los Angeles--in addition to those that operate from Miami; moves follow agreement between US and Cuba to begin working more closely to combat sharp rise in drug trafficking in and around island and to take other steps to foster more cooperation; fall well within limits on American policy set in 1992 and 1996 by laws that stiffened 37-year-old embargo on island (M)

1999-07-07.title: In Provincial Serbian Town, 10,000 Say 'No' to Milosevic
1999-07-07.abstract: More than 10,000 people in Uzice, Serbia, hold demonstration against Pres Slobodan Milosevic, defying police who declared the rally illegal; Zoran Djindjic, opposition leader, tells crowd that he fled country during Kosovo war because of death threats from Serbian paramilitary; says there will be daily demonstrations in cities across Serbia; says he expects Serbian Orthodox Church, which recently demanded Milosevic's resignation, to join opposition in calling for nationwide general strike; photo; map (M)

1999-07-08.title: 6 Are Killed in Ethnic Strife in Solomon Islands
1999-07-08.abstract: United Nations official Kevin M Kennedy say at least six people have died and 15,000 to 20,000 have fled homes in ethnic conflict on Guadalcanal, Solomon Islands, interview; says UN will back mediation effort and help aid refugees; refugees say small bands of lightly armed militants belonging to Gwale majority have driven most of minority population of Malaitan villagers from countryside to capital, Honiara, whose population is mainly from island of Malaita; UN sent mission in response to appeal from Prime Min Bartholomew Ulufa'alu, Malaitan, who asked Commonweath to mediate; Sitiveni Rabuka, Fiji's former Prime Minister, has been trying to negotiate end to violence; map (M)

1999-07-08.title: Brazil's Leader Undercut By His Quarreling Allies
1999-07-08.abstract: Brazilian Congress adjourns for midwinter recess after session marked by squabbling among four parties that supposedly back Pres Fernando Henrique Cardoso; infighting sapped his personal prestige and stalled progress on bills needed to keep International Monetary Fund economic rescue package on track; Cardoso has shown himself reluctant to exert authority when nominal allies misbehave or fail to keep promises; photo (M)

1999-07-08.title: Imprisoned Russian Writer Puts the Gulag on Line
1999-07-08.abstract: Grigory Pasko, military journalist and Russian Navy captain jailed after documenting Navy's practice of dumping nuclear waste at sea, chronicles dismal life in overcrowded and disease-infested prison on popular political news site on Internet; verdict in his long-running trial is expected this month; photo (M)

1999-07-08.title: For Books, It Pays To Surf Around
1999-07-08.abstract: PARIS—  People around the world who want to buy books in English know about Amazon.com and its arch-competitor, barnesandnoble.com. Both of those on-line booksellers do substantial mail-order business outside the United States. The Web has made available millions of books that were previously hard to come by outside English-speaking countries.

1999-07-08.title: All Nature Mourns Late Leader, North Korea Says
1999-07-08.abstract: North Korea's news media is going all out to hail 5th anniversary of Pres Kim Il Sung's death and pass mantle of authority to his son, Kim Jong Il; claim flowers are blossoming out of season to mark anniversary (M)

1999-07-09.title: World Briefing
1999-07-09.abstract: EUROPE    NORTHERN IRELAND: DUBLIN PLEA TO I.R.A. -- Prime Minister Bertie Ahern of Ireland urged the I.R.A. to make a ''clear, unambiguous statement'' that it intends to disarm under the terms of the Northern Ireland peace accord. This, he said, could ease the entry of members of the I.R.A.'s political wing, Sinn Fein, into a new Protestant-Catholic cabinet by next Thursday's deadline for establishment of the new body. The I.R.A., which has observed a cease-fire for two years, has repeatedly said it has no plans to disarm.   James F. Clarity (NYT)    BRITAIN: PINOCHET'S COST TO TAXPAYERS -- The Law Lords, England's highest court, ruled that Gen. Augusto Pinochet would have to pay little of the costs stemming from the legal actions since his arrest in London last October on a Spanish request to extradite him to Madrid to face charges of crimes against humanity. The seven-judge panel did not speak of money amounts, but its breakdown of the 83-year-old former Chilean dictator's responsibility for legal fees meant that most of the estimated $10 million the case has cost so far would be borne by British taxpayers.   Warren Hoge (NYT)

1999-07-09.title: 3,000 Rally Against Milosevic, to Only a Feeble Response
1999-07-09.abstract: Protesters against Pres Slobodan Milosevic gather in main square of small Serbian southern town of Prokuplje, and Government fails to stage a planned rally of its own in response; Alliance for Change, collection of pro-democracy parties, draws 3,000 to 4,000 people to its rally; photo (M)

1999-07-09.title: U.N. Backs Mine Expert Expelled by Iraq
1999-07-09.abstract: United Nations defends a mine-clearing expert who is expelled by Iraq; expresses concern about Baghdad's continuing attempts to bar employees of certain nationalities or groups from working in Iraq (M)

1999-07-09.title: Shafik al-Wazzan, 74, Leader Of Lebanon in Tumultuous 80's
1999-07-09.abstract: Shafik al-Wazzan, Prime Minister of Lebanon during tumultuous 1980's, dies at age 74; photo (M)

1999-07-09.title: Kazakhstan Blocks a Launching by Russia
1999-07-09.abstract: Government of Kazakhstan bars Russia from carrying out scheduled launching from Baikonur Cosmodrome; action comes three days after Russian rocket disintegrates over a grain-growing region (S)

1999-07-10.title: Barak's Quest Starts With Boost by Egypt
1999-07-10.abstract: Prime Min Ehud Barak promises that Israel with 'turn every stone' in quest for a broader Middle East peace, but cautions that it will be a few weeks before he will be ready to make his first moves; meets with Pres Hosni Mubarak of Egypt in Alexandria, who praises Barak as a friend and man of his word; photo (M)

1999-07-10.title: World Briefing
1999-07-10.abstract: EUROPE    BRITAIN: MAD COW BAN ENDING -- Officials expect the European Union's ban on Britain's worldwide beef exports stemming from a 1996 outbreak of mad cow disease to be lifted next week. The ban was imposed after the disease swept through British cattle herds and was linked to Creutzfeldt-Jakob Disease, which attacks the human brain. More than four million animals have been destroyed in an effort to stamp out the disease, which has accounted for 40 deaths.   Warren Hoge (NYT)

1999-07-10.title: Saudi's Visit To Arms Site In Pakistan Worries U.S.
1999-07-10.abstract: Clinton Administration is alarmed by the visit that Saudi Arabia's Defense Minister, Prince Sultan bin Abdelaziz al-Saud, made to Pakistani nuclear and missile facilities; officials say Saudis 'obfuscated' when asked about visit (M)

1999-07-10.title: European Union's Commission Is Revamped After a Scandal; a 'New Era' Is Promised
1999-07-10.abstract: Romano Prodi, president of European Union's Executive Commission, announces appointment of 19 commissioners and promises new era of change after the forced resignation of the entire previous commission amid accusations of corruption; among the new commissioners is Chris Patten of Britain, who will direct European Union's relations with foreign countries (M)

1999-07-10.title: Blair Spokesman Defends Alliance Briefings : A Key Adviser to NATO Assails Kosovo Coverage
1999-07-10.abstract: LONDON—  A key figure in the media campaign waged by NATO governments during the conflict in Kosovo criticized Western journalists on Friday for devoting as much coverage to alliance blunders and Serb civilian casualties as to the atrocities committed by Serb forces against ethnic Albanians.

1999-07-11.title: Kosovo Gypsies Now Among Vengeful Neighbors
1999-07-11.abstract: Nearly 5,000 Gypsies of Kosovo have sought shelter in schoolhouse in Kosovo Polje, fleeing vengeance of returning Albanians who accuse them of collaborating with Serbs in last months of violence and ethnic purges; photo (M)

1999-07-11.title: U.N. Plans Force in Congo After Cease-Fire
1999-07-11.abstract: United Nations is preparing to send large peacekeeping force to help stabilize Congo once cease-fire is signed, despite reservations by Clinton Administration (S)

1999-07-11.title: Past Military Rule's Abuse Is Haunting Brazil Today
1999-07-11.abstract: Nearly decade and half of democracy has erased abuses of military rule from memory of most Brazilians, but two cases dating from unpleasant 21-year period have suddenly been thrust into national spotlight, creating embarrassment for Pres Fernando Henrique Cardoso's Government (M)

1999-07-11.title: U.N. Says It Is Getting Farmers To Replace Their Drug Crops
1999-07-11.abstract: Pino Arlacchi, director of United Nations International Drug Control Program who has proposed eliminating opium poppy and coca leaf cultivation in 10 years, says his timetable for eradicating raw ingredients for heroin and cocaine around world is running ahead of schedule in some countries, but he acknowledges difficulties in others (M)

1999-07-11.title: India Says Its Has Evicted Most Pakistani Fighters in Kashmir
1999-07-11.abstract: Prime Min Atal Behari Vajpayee of India says military has chased out or killed most of Pakistani soldiers who have occupied strategic mountain tops on Indian side of Kashmir; Prime Min Nawaz Sharif of Pakistan continues efforts to convince men he insists are Islamic freedom fighters, not Pakistani soldiers, to withdraw voluntarily; Indian officials say that recently seized North Korean cargo ship was carrying makings of tactical surface-to-surface missiles destined for Pakistan (M)

1999-07-12.title: FOES REPORT PLAN TO BEGIN PULLOUT IN KASHMIR REGION
1999-07-12.abstract: India and Pakistan say rival forces battling for past two months over Indian state of Kashmir, will begin phased withdrawal, heralding an end to latest confrontation between two countries; move toward peace comes week after Pakistan's Prime Min Nawaz Sharif visited with Pres Clinton in Washington and promised to take concrete steps to end crisis; both nations have fought two wars over Kashmir since winning independence from Britain in 1947; map; photo (M)

1999-07-12.title: Not Quite a Triumph in Congo
1999-07-12.abstract: Analysis of political ramifications of failure of rebels in Congo to sign peace agreement aimed at ending civil war; photo; map (M)

1999-07-12.title: At Border, Arafat and Barak Meet to Build Road to Peace
1999-07-12.abstract: New Israeli Prime Min Ehud Barak meets with Palestinian leader Yasir Arafat in Gaza in first Israeli-Palestinian summit in seven months; both say new era of peacemaking lies ahead, in which they will be partners; Barak, meeting with Arafat just five days after taking office, expresses determination to overcome all obstacles; allays anxiety of Palestinians by promising to move quickly to carry out terms of Wye River peace agreement; does not discuss major Palestinian concern over recent expansion of Jewish settlements in West Bank; reiterates that there will be no new settlements and no demolition of existing ones; meeting signals radical shift in tense, hostile relations that marked tenure of Benjamin Netanyahu, Barak's successor; photo (M)

1999-07-12.title: Senator Blocking Holbrooke Calls Albright the Real Villain
1999-07-12.abstract: Republican Sen Chrles Grassley, who is blocking final Senate vote on Richard C Holbrooke's nomination to be chief American diplomat at UN, says Sec of State Madeleine K Albright is directly responsible for delay because of her treatment of State Dept employee Linda S Shenwick, who is described as whistle-blower (M)

1999-07-12.title: For Fourth Day, Kosovo Albanians March to Protest a Plan for Russian Peacekeepers
1999-07-12.abstract: Several thousand Kosovo Albanians march through Orahovac, Kosovo, on fourth day of peaceful protests against plans to station Russian troops among NATO peacekeepers; Albanians say Russian mercenaries fought as members of nationalist Serb militias that carried out campaign of killling, looting and burning across southwest corner of Kosovo and that Russians would be overly solicitous of handful of remaining Serbs; map (M)

1999-07-13.title: British Putting More Pressure On the I.R.A. To Disarm
1999-07-13.abstract: Britain pushes legislation aimed at obtaining Protestant support for new Northern Ireland government, that would require Irish Republican Army to follow enforceable disarmament timetable, as deadline approaches to reach agreement on new government for Ulster; issue of when and how largely Catholic IRA will disarm has been main objection raised by Protestants to carrying out Northern Ireland peace settlement, agreed to 14 months ago and implemented haltingly since then (M)

1999-07-13.title: U.S. Says It Has Fingerprints Of Embassy Bombing Suspects
1999-07-13.abstract: Federal prosecutors say fingerprints of two Egyptian associates of exiled Saudi terrorist Osama bin Laden were found on statements that took responsibility for bomb attacks against American embassies in Kenya and Tanzania in August 1998; both men, Ibrahim Hussein Eidarous and Abdel Mohammed Abdul Bari, were arrested in London on July 11 (M)

1999-07-13.title: India Holds Fire in Kashmir As Enemy Forces Pull Out
1999-07-13.abstract: India announces suspension of six-week military campaign against Pakistan-based fighters who occupied Himalayan peaks in disputed state of Kashmir; pledges not to attack fighters as long as they complete withdrawal by July 16; Indian and Pakistan officials say military commanders have agreed to terms and timetable of pullout of fighters, who Pakistanis say are Islamic militants and India maintains are mostly Pakistani soldiers (M)

1999-07-13.title: Iran Protests Spread to 18 Cities; Police Crack Down at University
1999-07-13.abstract: Most widespread and sustained protests since Iran's Islamic revolution two decades ago spreads throughout country; security police and vigilante supporters move to crush pro-democracy student demonstrators outside Teheran University; demonstrations reported in 18 cities and towns, including major cosmopolitan cities of Tabriz, Shiraz and Isfahan, and more traditional cities like Meshed and Yazd; reflect deep struggle over course of Islamic revolution; students are impatient with slow pace of reforms promised by Pres Mohammad Khatami; are not calling for change in Islamic system of government, but for quickening of movement toward democracy and rule of law; diehard Islamic revolutionaries, some in positions of power, believe country's moves toward democracy are betrayal of revolutionary purism; demonstrations follow passage by Parliament of tough new press law and closure of Salam, popular left-leaning Islamic newspaper; map; photos (M)

1999-07-13.title: TAIWAN PRESIDENT IMPLIES HIS ISLAND IS SOVEREIGN STATE
1999-07-13.abstract: Taiwan abandons political formula that has long helped avert war with China, declaring it will no longer adhere to 'One China' principle that Chinese mainland and Taiwan are two parts of same country; Beijing voices outrage; accuses Pres Lee Teng-hui of taking extremely dangerous steps and warns of possible use of force; surprising and sudden shift risks badly souring Taiwan's relations with China and strains already troubled US-China relationship; represents step away from conceptual umbrella of inevitable reunification between China and Taiwan--which has been basic condition of all talks between them (M)

1999-07-14.title: 'What Is China?' Taiwan's New Answer Is Puzzling
1999-07-14.abstract: News analysis of Taiwan Pres Lee Teng-hui's surprise statement that his Government no longer stands by premise that Taiwan and mainland are parts of same country; photo; even hint that Taiwan is moving toward formal declaration of independence rings loud alarm bells in Beijing, and shift inevitably complicates US-China relations, as well as election politics in Taiwan; Lee is expected to step down next spring after 12 years in office and apparently wants to cement his legacy as father of modern Taiwan separate from mainland (S)

1999-07-14.title: Serbian Town Bombed by NATO Fears Effects of Toxic Chemicals
1999-07-14.abstract: Serbs living in Pancevo fear effects of toxic chemicals released from petrochemical and fertilizer factories that were bombed during NATO air campaign over Kosovo; photo; map; residents report blistering rashes from touching soil and stomach ailments from eating river fish and vegetables or drinking tap water; children, many of whom were sent to Slovakia by Red Cross to escape noxious gases, still suffer headaches and dizziness, and doctors report twice as many miscarriages as last year; authorities admit testing equipment is outdated; UN sets up investigation under Finnish environmental official Pakka Haavisto; he says biggest danger now is that ground water and Danube River are directly polluted; air strikes against industrial complex in April unleashed tons of chemicals as NATO tried to cut off Milosevic Government's resources; Pancevo has 130,000 residents, many of Hungarian and Croatian descent, and has been center of opposition to Slobodan Milosevic (M)

1999-07-14.title: Serb Opposition Leader Now Plans Protests
1999-07-14.abstract: Serb opposition leader Vuk Draskovic now says he will call supporters into streets to topple Pres Slobodan Milosevic (S)

1999-07-14.title: U.S. Asking Taiwan to Explain Its Policy After Uproar
1999-07-14.abstract: US asks Taiwan to explain its sudden declaration that it no longer stands by 'one China' policy; envoy Darryl Johnson will tell Pres Lee Teng-hui that his startling announcement is unhelpful (S)

1999-07-14.title: Swiss Step Up Their Scrutiny On Transfers Of Big Money
1999-07-14.abstract: Swiss police authorities report sharp increase in number of reports of suspected money laundering under new law mandating stricter accountability by banks (S)

1999-07-15.title: Britain and Argentina to Allow Commercial Falkland Flights
1999-07-15.abstract: Argentine and British Governments agree to reinstate commercial flights between Argentina and Falkland Islands for first time since war over archipelago in 1982; agreement is hailed by Argentine officials as major diplomatic victory; Britain says agreement does not resolve dispute over which country has right to islands, which have been occupied by British citizens for more than century; Argentina says it opens way to improved ties between both two former enemies, which only restored diplomatic relations in 1990; Pres Carlos Saul Menem says final objective is to regain sovereignty over islands, which Argentina calls Malvinas (M)

1999-07-15.title: Pakistan-Backed Force Leaves Indian Kashmir
1999-07-15.abstract: Indian officials say withdrawal of Pakistani-backed forces from Indian-controlled area of Kashmir is on schedule to be finished by July 16 deadline; accuse Pakistan of sponsoring guerrilla attack on paramilitary camp near Srinigar on July 13; map (M)

1999-07-15.title: On Barak Visit, Is It First Lady Hillary Clinton, or Candidate Hillary Clinton?
1999-07-15.abstract: White House and State Dept struggle to adjust schedules during upcoming visit of new Israeli Prime Min Ehud Barak that fit not only into Pres Clinton's calendar but that of Hillary Rodham Clinton, who is playing unprecedented dual roles of First Lady and likely Senate candidate in New York; situation underscores growing challenge facing White House over next 15 months--of harmonizing not only political and policy messages of President, Vice Pres and First Lady, but also managing complex scheduling and logistics involved in Presidency and campaigns of Mrs Clinton and Al Gore (M)

1999-07-15.title: China Proclaims It Designed Its Own Neutron Bomb
1999-07-15.abstract: China says it has successfully developed design technology to make neutron bombs and miniaturized nuclear weapons on its own, first time it has made such assertions publicly; announcement seems timed as pointed reminder of its military capabilities, coming just days after Taiwan shifted stance toward China; rebuts Cox Committee report issued by Congressional committee in May, which accuses China of systematically stealing American nuclear technology--such as information on designs for neutron bomb and for W-88, most advanced miniaturized warhead; China says report is misleading, full of lies and racist (M)

1999-07-15.title: At a Hopeful Moment, Feud Tears at Mexico's Left
1999-07-15.abstract: Mexico's social democrats, represented by Democratic Revolutionary Party, is on binge of self-destruction at time when it stands fighting chance of both winning Presidential election in 2000 and actually taking office; has been hamstrung by infighting among top party leaders, party's inability to organize clean election of its own and falling popularity of Mexico City Mayor Cuauhtemoc Cardenas, party's co-founder and its likely Presidential nominee; photo (M)

1999-07-16.title: 'Kid With a New Toy' (and a Red Face)
1999-07-16.abstract: Comment on misunderstanding that arises over Pres Clinton's statement that he is 'eager as a kid with a new toy' to meet with Israeli Prime Min Ehud Barak (S)

1999-07-16.title: Town Hushed in '95 Crackdown Sees No Reason to Join Iran Riots
1999-07-16.abstract: Town of Islamshahr, Iran, scene of antigovernment riots in April of 1995, is subdued this time around, with people reluctant to discuss unrest in nation; in their own way, young men of Islamshahr are just as disaffected from conservative Islamic religious Government as students in Teheran; they share same desires for personal freedoms; their priorities are different, however; men of Islamshahr want jobs; map (M)

1999-07-16.title: U.S. Tries to Play Down China's Announcement on Bomb
1999-07-16.abstract: Clinton Administration, seeking to contain growing crisis in Asia, tries to play down timing and significance of China's declaration that it had developed on its own the ability to make neutron bombs and miniature atomic weapons; but Congressional Republicans say Beijing's assertion actually validates Congressional inquiry that accused China of stealing American nuclear secrets and was timed precisely to intimidate Taiwan (M)

1999-07-16.title: Prosecutor in Pedophile Case Kills Himself : Belgian Official's Death Puts Top Cases On Hold
1999-07-16.abstract: BRUSSELS—  The death of a top prosecutor, apparently as the result of suicide, threw doubts Thursday over Belgium's two most prominent criminal investigations and forced Belgians to revisit some of the darker moments of their country's recent history.

1999-07-16.title: One Agreement in Ulster: Assembly Session Is a Farce
1999-07-16.abstract: What is supposed to have been most important day in evolving Northern Ireland peace agreement becomes scene of chaos, farce and recrimination; new Northern Ireland Assembly is shut down indefinitely; British Govt announces that whole peace agreement is on hold; David Trimble, head of main Protestant party, and his 28 Ulster Unionist colleagues boycott Assembly session, which was scheduled to nominate cabinet ministers; Trimble says his party could not share power with Sinn Fein unless IRA begins to disarm; Deputy First Min Seamus Mallon of moderate Catholic Social Democratic and Labor Party reacts with fury, announcing he is resigning; photo (M)

1999-07-17.title: Fears of Military Confrontation With China Shake Taiwan Financial Markets
1999-07-17.abstract: Fear of military confrontation with China sweeps Taiwan's financial markets, causing steep stock price plunge; jitters spread across region, with stock markets dropping in Japan, Hong Kong and Singapore; domestic politics deepen instability, with James Soong announcing that he will break away from governing party and run for president as independent; some analysts fear prolonged instability could derail economy (M)

1999-07-17.title: WORLD BRIEFING
1999-07-17.abstract: EUROPE    FRANCE: LIBYA PAYS FOR BOMBING PLANE -- Libya has transferred $31 million to France to compensate families of the 171 people killed when a French airliner was blown up over the African desert in 1989, officials said. A French court that convicted six high-ranking Libyan officials in absentia this year ordered Libya to pay compensation for the bombing, which occurred at a time of tension between the two countries over Chad. The French Foreign Ministry said the payment was acknowledgment by Libya that its officials were responsible for the deaths.   Craig R. Whitney (NYT)

1999-07-17.title: Iran's View of U.S.: Evil, Bumbling or Unimportant
1999-07-17.abstract: Official Iranian view of United States is contradictory, with US presented both as evil mastermind behind every misfortune and as inept superpower unable to destroy Islamic Republic; third view, espoused by reformists newspapers that back Pres Mohammad Khatami, is that Iran can no longer play role of victim and must solve own problems; photo (M)

1999-07-17.title: Irish Need Comply
1999-07-17.abstract: Breakdown of Northern Ireland peace accord may be more loss of momentum than abandonment of purpose; has disclosed both political vacuum feared as opportunity for violence and reassuring new stability; paramilitary groups have maintained their cease-fires and economic development continues (M)

1999-07-17.title: Clinton and Barak Drawn Together by the Politics of Peace
1999-07-17.abstract: Pres Clinton describes his Camp David meeting with Israeli Prime Min Ehud Barak, phone interview; hails Barak's commitment to peace and his awareness of risks of peace process (M)

1999-07-18.title: With Kinder Face, the Left Is Blossoming in Uruguay
1999-07-18.abstract: Tabare Vazquez, who is one of Uruguay's leading oncologists, and former mayor of capital, Montevideo, is waging busy campaign for President, leading leftist coalition of former Tupamaro guerrillas, Communists, Socialists and Trotskyites; political leaders of Uruguay's two traditional centrist parties, including Pres Julio Maria Sanguinetti, contend Vazquez is really a Marxist wolf in disguise who would destroy Uruguayan economy; polls indicate that Vazquez stands head-to-head with Jorge Batlle of center-left Colorado Party with three months remaining before first-round vote; former Pres Luis Alberto Lacalle of center-right Blanco Party is falling well behind; Vazquez photo; map (M)

1999-07-18.title: Key Opposition Leader Joins Rally Asking Milosevic to Resign
1999-07-18.abstract: Vuk Draskovic, a leading opposition politician, appears for first time at rally calling for resignation of Yugoslav Pres Slobodan Milosevic, and formation of transitional government leading to new electoins; Draskovic urges festive crowd of about 20,000 people not to seek revenge or pursue a violent path that could lead to 'civil war' (M)

1999-07-18.title: INQUIRY ESTIMATES SERB DRIVE KILLED 10,000 IN KOSOVO
1999-07-18.abstract: At least 10,000 people believed to be slaughtered by Serbian forces during three-month campaign to drive Albanians from Kosovo; war crimes investigators, NATO peacekeeping troops and aid agencies struggle to keep up with fresh reports each day of newly discovered bodies and graves; State Dept had estimated death toll to be 4,600 people in May, before international war crimes tribunal in The Hague indicted Pres Slobodan Milosevic and four top aides on charges of crimes against humanity; war crimes investigators say true toll may never be known, because some villagers have buried their dead without informing Western officials, and because many deaths went unrecorded in chaotic press of events when peacekeepers and United Nations relief workers first arrived; some bodies may have been burned or carried off by Serbs in effort to destroy evidence; photos; map (M)

1999-07-18.title: Talks Fail to End Mexico University Strike
1999-07-18.abstract: Talks between striking students and authorities from Mexico's largest university aimed at settling 88-day-old shutdown have collapsed, making possibility of police action to open campus more real (S)

1999-07-18.title: Israeli's Talk With Clinton: Syria Accord Back in View
1999-07-18.abstract: News analysis of first round of meetings in Washington between Pres Clinton and new Israeli Prime Min Ehud Barak; officials familiar with their conversations note that how to reach an agreement with Syria, one of last Arab countries still at war with Israel, dominated their discussions; Clinton Adm officials believe that a deal with Syrian Pres Hafez al-Assad is doable but that it will require involvement of highest-level American officials (M)

1999-07-19.title: Foe Suggests Giving Milosevic Immunity To Get Him to Quit
1999-07-19.abstract: Vuk Draskovic, leading Serbian opposition politician, says best way to get Pres Slobodan Milosevic out of power is to seduce him to resign by promising immunity from arrest or extradition for war crimes; says he will soon name shadow Yugoslav government of experts led by prime minister selected by Milo Djukanovic, democratic leader of Montenegro; will press Milosevic through public rallies to accept government and to call new elections in three to six months while opposition works to attract disaffected Milosevic supporters; others in opposition feel time is right to confront and oust Milosevic, not to encourage him; senior opposition figures remain intensely competitive, and their lack of unity makes it more likely that if Milosevic falls it will be through chaotic outpouring of discontent; map; photo (M)

1999-07-19.title: South Africa Confronts Brutalities Of One Man
1999-07-19.abstract: Grisly details of more than 100 instances of torture and killings by Eugene de Kock, South Africa's most notorious apartheid-era henchman, are contained in amnesty application pending before Truth and Reconciliation Commission; many South Africans believe de Kock has still not told entire truth and may be holding back details in effort to make himself look more like soldier than monster, even though confession runs for more than 4,000 pages and is by far largest ever received by commission; de Kock, who headed counterterrorism unit from 1985 to 1993, is serving two life terms plus 212 years for range of charges; has written book on his life, lashing out at generals who he says abandoned him after he finished their dirty work (M)

1999-07-19.title: Israel Will Buy 50 F-16's In Its Biggest Arms Deal Ever
1999-07-19.abstract: Israel confirms plans to add at least 50 more F-16's to already formidable jet fighter fleet; deal signals determination to maintain decisive regional air power superiority even as Israel pursues peace deals with its Middle Eastern neighbors; Prime Min Ehud Barak tells American officials that Israel wishes to but 50 new planes, a $2.5 billion order, with option to purchase 60 more for additional $2 billion; Israel already has 250 F-16's--largest fleet outside US (M)

1999-07-19.title: War in Congo Rattles On Despite Accord
1999-07-19.abstract: Fighting continues in Congo a week after signing of cease-fire; rebels report capturing more territory, and countries embroiled in year-old conflict trade accusations over violations; map (M)

1999-07-19.title: Cortona Journal; Act 2: Quest for Tuscan Pasta and Great Plastering
1999-07-19.abstract: Frances Mayes and her husband, Ed, gain cult following as result of Under the Tuscan Sun and Bella Tuscany, her two best-selling books describing their restoration of an 18th-century villa in Cortona, Italy; 30 to 40 travelers from US and other countries stop at gates the villa each day during high travel season to look and to seek autographs; American fixation with crumbling villas and recipes for toasted bread delights and bewilders people of Cortona; photo; map (M)

1999-07-20.title: Bailout Audit in Mexico Cites $7.7 Billion in Dubious Loans
1999-07-20.abstract: A Canadian auditor presented a report today about the Mexican Government's rescue of its banks concluding that about $7.7 billion of the loans absorbed by the bailout were highly irregular or plainly illegal.     Michael Mackey, the accountant hired by the Mexican Congress to  review the bailout, found that bank executives made billion-dollar loans to themselves that they never repaid; that banks lent millions of dollars to investors to buy shares in the same banks, and that top bankers made large loans to friends and family without as much as a credit study.

1999-07-20.title: WORLD BRIEFING
1999-07-20.abstract: EUROPE    BRITAIN: CUTTING BACK ON ROYALTY -- Britain plans to cut security for some members of the royal family as a way of reducing costs. Queen Elizabeth has asked the Government to review ways of lowering the $47 million annual bill for protection. Steps being considered include the removal of motorcycle outriders that escort royal limousines around London and the replacement of policemen patrolling homes with civilian guards.   Warren Hoge (NYT)    RUSSIA: BATTLES WITH CHECHENS -- Chechen fighters attacked Russian police posts and patrols, killing two commanding officers, wounding seven other policemen and provoking strikes by Russian artillery and helicopter gunships, Russian news outlets reported. The fighting along Chechnya's borders followed a weekend of skirmishes in which Chechen rebels killed four policemen and four civilians.   (AP)

1999-07-20.title: Graft Cases Remind South Koreans of Bad Old Days
1999-07-20.abstract: Corruption has long been something of a national pastime in South Korea. With few laws and little Government effort to prosecute offenders, many Koreans have come to regard bribes, payoffs and kickbacks as part of political life.    But that was all supposed to change with the election of Kim Dae Jung as President in December 1997. Mr. Kim, a former political dissident, campaigned on a platform of stamping out corruption and reforming South Korea's faltering economy.

1999-07-20.title: Jakarta Reportedly Predicts East Timor Will Vote to Split Off
1999-07-20.abstract: Indonesia is preparing to relinquish control of East Timor after 23 years of occupation, and it believes that independence advocates are highly likely to win a referendum next month, says a supposedly authentic internal Government report that has been made available to reporters by advocates of independence.    The report predicted violence if independence forces won and anticipated that non-Timorese who control many businesses would leave.

1999-07-20.title: Soon to Bow Out, Argentine Brushes Off the Boos
1999-07-20.abstract: The wild mutton-chop sideburns are gone. The televised cheek-to-cheek tangos with marquee entertainers  are a thing of the past. And when President Carlos Saul Menem does take the occasional spin in a hot sports car these days, his bodyguards no longer need to make death-defying weaves through traffic to chase after him.    At age 69 and in his final five months in office, Mr. Menem still makes good copy -- but now the press attention is focused on the presidential psyche. Argentine magazines, quoting anonymous sources, report that Mr. Menem is depressed, lonely and bitter because many of his closest allies, including his hand-picked Vice President, have abandoned him.

1999-07-21.title: Europe's Rights Court Taking On Increasingly Sensitive Cases
1999-07-21.abstract: European Court of Human Rights, Strasbourg, France, which serves 41-nation Council of Europe, is raising its visibility with high-profile cases; Luzius Wildhaber, court president, comments (M)

1999-07-21.title: But Couture Outfits Grab Public's Attention : Wild, and Unwearable
1999-07-21.abstract: PARIS—  The Phantom Menace has struck high fashion with designers at this week's couture shows divided between attention-grabbing futurism and clothes for the terrestrial world.

1999-07-21.title: Belgrade Officials Plan Back Pay for Protesting Reservists
1999-07-21.abstract: Yugoslav Government plans to authorize back pay for reservists, pensioners and teachers in bid to assuage popular grievances and stop wave of protests by army reservists unpaid for months spent in Kosovo; new payments are likely to spur inflation in damaged economy; map; photo (M)

1999-07-21.title: Hugh Fish, 76, Who Made Thames So Clean the Salmon Came Back
1999-07-21.abstract: Hugh Fish, British environmentalist whose love of pristine rivers helped clean up Thames, dies at age 76 (S)

1999-07-21.title: Beijing Detains Leaders of Sect, Watchdog Says
1999-07-21.abstract: Hong Kong-based human rights group charges that China has detained at least 70 Buddhist Law spiritual movement leaders in 14 cities; says police searched leaders' homes, confiscating books about rapidly growing movement and addresses of people who attended meetings; group, known in Chinese as Falun Gong, could be potent force in nation facing dislocating social and economic changes; was able to mobilize thousands of followers without warning for April protest; several hundred members stage protests in Dalian and two smaller cities; map (M)

1999-07-22.title: Runaway Banks Without Brakes: Mexico's $71 Billion Lesson
1999-07-22.abstract: Battle over assigning blame for costly bailout of Mexico's privatized banks comes to point of closure with report commissioned by Congress in which Canadian auditor, Michael W Mackey, examines banking system performance for last two decades and record of Government rescue program since 1994; report describes system in which small business elite controls banks and many of companies seeking loans; cites lack of effective supervision of system in 1991, when Government began to sell banks; notes many purchasers lacked expertise to run banks and failed to hire competent management; produces no evidence that nay Government official broke law or was grossly negligent; photo (M)

1999-07-22.title: Mahaheng Amatsho Journal; Seer's Life: Win a Few, Lose a Few, Take an Aspirin
1999-07-22.abstract: Interview with Mmatsepo, healer and spiritual counselor accorded much reverence in Lesotho, where she is seer to kings; she mixes traditionalism with modernism; map; photo (M)

1999-07-22.title: U.N. Says It's NATO That Lags in Kosovo
1999-07-22.abstract: United Nations Asst Sec Gen John Ruggie, stung by charges by United States military leaders that United Nations is moving too slowly in creating police force and civil administration in Kosovo, notes NATO members have not yet deployed all soldiers pledged; holds UN is moving according to plan, deploying people and resources at 'unprecedented' pace; says full UN police force will be in place throughout Kosovo by Nov or Dec (M)

1999-07-22.title: North Korea, Ignoring Warnings, Proceeds With Plans to Test-Fire Missile
1999-07-22.abstract: North Korea, defiant in face of stern warnings from United States, Japan and South Korea, reportedly proceeds with plans to test-fire long-range ballistic missile; concern about missile program is rising across Asia and in US; experts say missile is likely to set off arms race in Northeast Asia (M)

1999-07-22.title: WORLD BRIEFING
1999-07-22.abstract: EUROPE    ITALY: NO MORE REFUGEES  Italy said it would resume treating people entering from Yugoslavia without visas as illegal immigrants, not refugees. During the NATO air strikes, Italy had pledged to harbor up to 10,000 Kosovo refugees. But thousands of immigrants from the Balkans arrive every summer. This week alone, about 1,300 Kosovo Gypsies came to the southern port of Brindisi.    Alessandra Stanley (NYT)

1999-07-23.title: Tardy Kosovo Rebels Force Extension of Arms Deadline
1999-07-23.abstract: Failure by Kosovo Liberation Army to turn in sufficient number of its heavy weapons, including mortars and antitank and antiaircraft guns, forces NATO commanders to extend deadline for compliance with a disarmament agreement; NATO officials say it is apparent that rebel commanders have hidden large stockpiles, and that peacekeepers fear growing divisions among rebels could lead to factional fighting; map (M)

1999-07-23.title: I.R.A. Blames Britain for Breakdown of Peace Accord
1999-07-23.abstract: Irish Republican Army blames British Government for last week's breakdown of Northern Ireland peace settlement, saying hopes for accord are now 'substantially diminished'; photo (M)

1999-07-23.title: China Steps Up Its Drive to Halt Dissident Sect
1999-07-23.abstract: China steps up its offensive against Buddhist Law movement, announcing ban on organization and unleashing barrage of charges against its founder that reveal how seriously Government takes sect as a challenge to its control; Government's ban on group, known in Chinese as Falun Gong, has been rumored since police rounded up more than 100 leaders of sect in several Chinese cities this week; but ferocity of Beijing's campaign--aimed at a group of mainly middle-aged people who practice form of Chinese breathing exercises and meditation--suggests that it regards Falun Gong as more than a movement of physical and moral uplift; at a time when China's economy is slowing and social unrest is rising, officials view amorphous but fast-growing sect as a dangerous political force; photo of several Falun Gong members exercising (M)

1999-07-23.title: C.I.A. Says Chinese Embassy Bombing Resulted From Its Sole Attempt to Pick Targets
1999-07-23.abstract: CIA Director George J Tenet asserts that his agency had selected just one target of 11-week air war over Yugoslavia, and its decision led to accidental bombing of Chinese Embassy in May, House Intelligence Committee hearing; an American official says that after strike on May 7, which killed three Chinese and wounded at least 20 others, CIA decided it should go back to its usual business of spying (M)

1999-07-23.title: Another U.S. Rate Hike?Greenspan Hints at 'Yes'
1999-07-23.abstract: NEW YORK—  Alan Greenspan, the Federal Reserve Board chairman, sounded a hawkish note in congressional testimony on Thursday, saying the U.S. economy seemed to be growing too fast and indicating the central bank was likely to raise interest rates to slow it down.

1999-07-24.title: New Russians Spread Money on Riviera, but Lack the Panache of the Czars
1999-07-24.abstract: Russians are big spenders along French Riviera, buying up apartments and designer clothes and booking five-star hotel suites and limousines for weeks at a time; they charter yachts and prefer Champagne to vodka; whereas pre-Revolutionary Russians brought their own servants to Riviera, modern-day Russians come with bodyguards; map (M)

1999-07-24.title: U.S. Quietly Resuming Aid for Some Anti-Milosevic Groups
1999-07-24.abstract: Clinton Administration quietly lifts restrictions on support for Serbian opposition groups and frees aid for opposition political parties, the independent media, lawyers and other groups promoting democracy in Yugoslavia (M)

1999-07-24.title: Banned Movement's Head Urges Talks With China
1999-07-24.abstract: Li Hongzhi, leader of China's fast-spreading Falun Gong spiritual movement, says in New York that his group, now banned by Government, has no political ambitions; calls on Government to engage in dialogue; fears Government crackdown will do damage to the state and human rights and attract attention of internation public opinion; photo of police patrolling Beijing's Tiananmen Square (M)

1999-07-24.title: Condoms for Women Gain Approval Among Africans
1999-07-24.abstract: Female condom, seldom used in West, is catching on in South Africa, finding favor with men as well as women, and it is slowly becoming an important new weapon in fight against AIDS epidemic; United Nations AIDS and other organizations working to persuade more women to try the female condom are first approaching prostitutes and other women with multiple partners, since persuading them to use condoms has notably reduced rates of AIDS infection; female condom, properly used, is believed to be as good as or better than the male condom at preventing infection and is slightly less effective than male condom at preventing pregnancy; photo (M)

1999-07-24.title: Serbian Opposition Leaders Underwhelm Large Rally
1999-07-24.abstract: Several thousand Serbs gather in Pancevo to call for removal of Yugoslav President, Slobodan Milosevic, but the rally is small, decent, quiet, polite and boring; speakers represent various parts of umbrella Alliance for Change, whose most popular figure is 80-year-old Dragoslav Avramovic, but he is resisting US pressure to take a leading political role in effort to unify opposition, still badly split between Alliance and the larger, more populist Serbian Renewal Movement of Vuk Draskovic; photo (M)

1999-07-25.title: Slaying of Serbs Sets Back Effort for Kosovo Peace
1999-07-25.abstract: Slaying of 14 Serbian farmers near Gracko, worst single assault against dwindling pockets of Serbian civilians left in Kosovo since June arrival of NATO troops, seems likely to further undermine international efforts to keep Serbian minority from fleeing Kosovo; over 80,000 of estimated 200,000 Serbs living in Kosovo have fled since mid-June; soldiers have proved powerless so far to stop what Kosovo Serbs call daily harassment, with repeated rumors of kidnappings and killings; NATO and Kosovo Liberation Army officials score killings; Bernard Koucher, United Nations transitional administrator, vows gunmen will be arrested and tried; map; photos (M)

1999-07-25.title: Russian Gangsters Exploit Capitalism To Increase Profits
1999-07-25.abstract: Current and former American officials cite money laundering efforts of Russian organized crime figure Semyon Yukovich Mogilevich in North American equities market as first example of manipulation and infiltration of world financial markets by Russian organized crime; describe how he set up YBM Magnex, publicly traded United States company, as sophisticated cover for money laundering; fear similar schemes await exposure; YBM pleaded guilty to stock fraud; graph of YBM stock performance (M)

1999-07-25.title: Serb Opposition Leader Criticizes NATO Forces in Kosovo
1999-07-25.abstract: Serbian opposition leader Vuk Draskovic charges NATO forces allow ethnic purging of Serbs from Kosovo and creation of Albanian state in province, rally, Nis, Serbia; deplores slaying of 14 Serbian farmers in Gracko, Kosovo; holds it will spur anti-Western feelings in Serbia and hurt political opposition to Pres Slobodan Milosevic; blames Milosevic for tragedy of Kosovo; his aides say rally will be last for some time because time is not yet ripe for ousting Milosevic; rival opposition group plans to continue holding rallies (M)

1999-07-25.title: Swiss Reveal Secret World: Their Defense From Nazis
1999-07-25.abstract: Switzerland, straining under cost of maintaining vast network of bunkers built during World War II as defense against German invasion, has decided to declassify secret complexes, and even start to sell some off; built chain of underground fortresses in response to June 1940 German entry into Paris; thousands of people worked night and day to tunnel into mountains and build hiding places for arms and men (M)

1999-07-25.title: U.S. to Seek To Alter Plan For Pursuing War Crimes
1999-07-25.abstract: United States is expected to continue its drive against politically inspired war-crimes prosecutions at conference on proposed International Criminal Court set for United Nations; meeting of representatives of about 120 nations that have approved court's formation will discuss outstanding technical issues; US, concerned its citizens might suffer frivolous or politically inspired prosecution, voted against treaty setting up new tribunal (M)

1999-07-26.title: In Scenes Of Tumult, Moroccans Bury King
1999-07-26.abstract: Pres Clinton and French Pres Jacques Chirac lead assemblage of world leaders at funeral of King Hassan II of Morocco in capital of Rabat; join hundreds of thousands of mourners in three-mile walk to cemetery; Clinton praises Hassan as man of wisdom and stature who was never embittered by dangers he faced; crush of funeral procession gives rise to Secret Service concerns about Clinton's safety; other members of US delegation include First Lady Hillary Rodham Clinton and daughter Chelsea, former Pres George Bush, former Secretaries of State Warren Christopher and James A Baker 3d; Israeli Prime Min Ehud Barak and Palestinian leader Yasir Arafat attend; Mrs Clinton is granted audience with King Mohammed VI, Hassan's 36-year-old son and successor, in rare honor for woman; she pays condolence call on Hassan's widow and other women of family; scene in Rabat described; photos (M)

1999-07-26.title: Chechen Group Trying to Ransom U.S. Aid Worker's Remains
1999-07-26.abstract: Chechen group, in new development surrounding disappearance of US aid worker Fred Cluny in war-torn Chechnya in 1995 during relief mission, claims to have his body and is demanding large ransom for its return; Russian authorities tell US group has turned over critical piece of evidence, namely, photograph that appears to show metal pin inserted in Cluny's leg after accident years ago; family rules out paying ransom; says doing so could endanger abduction of aid workers and might even compensate his killers; son says father adamantly opposed such action; Cluny went to Chechnya in 1995 on relief mission sponsored by billionaire investor George Soros; maps (M)

1999-07-26.title: Memo From Lahore; Editor Held 25 Days Finds Nightmare Never Ends
1999-07-26.abstract: Ordeal of Najam Sethi, outspoken Pakistani journalist critical of Govt, continues; he was released from prison after 25 days in detention after charges of sedition were dropped, but currently faces income tax probe and possible revocation of standing of Muslim and loss of voting rights; his passport has been confiscated; treatment has left many Pakistani journalists wary of criticizing Govt; map; photo (M)

1999-07-26.title: Hunger Ravages Angolans in Renewed Civil War
1999-07-26.abstract: Starvation takes hold in Huambo, Angola, little more than six months after rebel forces regained control over almost 70 percent of country; fighting has forced more than million Angolans off land and into cities, where aid organizations press efforts to dole out food to most needy; workers say appeals for more food are being ignored, even as shelling of local airport often forces planes full of grain to turn away; say indifference to life is new phenomenon prevalent among population, with many refugees having little desire to survive anymore; are demanding that malnourished children eat at distribution stations because parents are so desperate that they steal from own children; situation in and around city described; renewed fighting is crude struggle over money and power; both sides are well financed, with Govt controlling oil territories and Unita rebel organization diamonds, reportedly with portfolio of carefully invested stocks and bonds worth $4 billion; map; photo (M)

1999-07-26.title: U.S. AND CHINA SAY THEY ARE MENDING POST-BOMBING RIFT
1999-07-26.abstract: US and China say tattered relationship has been improved after first high-level meeting since bombing of Chinese Embassy in Belgrade during NATO air strikes; say Pres Clinton will meet with Pres Jiang Zemin in September at Asia-Pacific Economic Cooperation forum in New Zealand; Sec of State Madeleine K Albright, who met with Chinese Foreign Min Tan Jiaxuan in Singapore at annual meeting of Assn of Southeast Asian Nations, says tensions have eased and that level of communications has been restored; Taiwan was hot topic of discussion; Albright pointedly criticizes at news conference comments by Taiwan Pres Lee Teng-hui renouncing 'one China' policy, which says Chinese mainland and Taiwan are separate parts of same country; reportedly expressed to Tang concern about rights of free assembly and expression in light of China's crackdown against followers of Buddhist Law movement; reportedly made little progress on China's demand for compensation for bombing of Embassy in Belgrade; photo (M)

1999-07-27.title: Albright and Ivanov Confer; Try to Mend Ties After Kosovo
1999-07-27.abstract: Secretary of State Madeliene K Albright confers with Russian Foreign Min Igor S Ivanov at ASEAN annual meeting in Singapore in effort to repair damage done to Moscow-Washington relationship by war in Kosovo, Serbia; both say ties should be repaired quickly and that efforts should be redoubled to further relationship; Ivanov says Russia will press for passage of Start II arms reduction treaty when Parliament convenes in September; Albright says discussions will soon begin on amending 1972 Anti-Ballistic Missile Treaty, which calls on both sides to reduce nuclear arsenals; US wants changes that would allow it to begin work on anti-missile system announced by Pres Clinton in January (M)

1999-07-27.title: WORLD BRIEFING
1999-07-27.abstract: ASIA    KASHMIR: INDIA SAYS INVADERS LEFT  The Indian military said it had pushed the last Pakistan-based forces from Indian-held territory in Kashmir. Most infiltrators had withdrawn earlier this month after Pakistan's Prime Minister, Nawaz Sharif, had called on them to do so, but Indian officials said a few pockets of fighters had remained close to the so-called Line of Control, which divides the portions of Kashmir that are held by India and Pakistan.   Celia Dugger (NYT)    INDONESIA: VOTE RESULTS DELAYED, AGAIN -- In what one election official called ''a very unfunny political joke,'' an announcement of the final results of the June 7 general election was postponed once more when representatives of losing parties refused to endorse the count. A supervisory committee will now review the results and officials said the delay could set back Indonesia's timetable for naming a new President by the end of the year.   Seth Mydans (NYT)

1999-07-27.title: U.S. Anti-Drug Chief, in Colombia, Speaks of 'Regional Crisis'
1999-07-27.abstract: Gen Barry R McCaffrey, Clinton Administration drug czar, arrives in Bogota, Colombia, to assess what he says is deepening regional crisis which Latin American governments fear will lead to American intervention; is accompanied by State Dept and military officials; visit comes 10 days after Colombian Government asked US for $500 in additional aid over next two years to combat left-wing guerrilla groups, which it says are deeply involved in drug trade; McCaffrey says it is in US national security interest to help in ending civil conflict in Colombia so country can be free to rid itself of narcotics traffickers (M)

1999-07-27.title: New Hospitality: Israeli Deputies Greet Palestinian
1999-07-27.abstract: Ahmed Qurei, Speaker of Palestinian Legislative Council, visits Israel as guest of Israeli Parliament; receives warm welcome from more dovish lawmakers; is first Palestinian to enter Parliament as formal guest; was invited by Avraham Burg, new activist Speaker of Israeli Parliament; both comment; photo (M)

1999-07-27.title: Mexican Opposition Runs Its Own Clean Election
1999-07-27.abstract: Mexico's left-of-center opposition, Party of the Democratic Revolution, holds second election to select party president; Sen Amalia Garcia Medina leads in preliminary count with 54 percent of vote; March 14 election was annulled amid allegations of corruption (M)

1999-07-28.title: North Korea Urged Again Not to Test New Missile
1999-07-28.abstract: State Sec Madeleine K Albright, in Singapore for Association of Southeast Asian Nations meeting, joins with South Korea and Japan in appealing to North Korea to accept engagement with world rather than risk consequences of long-range missile test; Min Masahiko Komura warns test would likely spur Japan to withdraw $1 billion it recently allocated for Korean Energy Development Organization (M)

1999-07-28.title: In Congo War's Wake, a Massacre of the Wildlife
1999-07-28.abstract: Five-year war in Congo is destroying region's distincly beautiful wildlife; disrupts complex dynamic among animals, local people and rich foreign tourists that some conservationists believe is best hope for saving Africa's dwindling wildlife; poaching and hostility toward Kahuzi-Biega National Park is on rise as tourist absence drives down local economy; maps; photo (M)

1999-07-28.title: U.S. Starts to Fly Eager Kosovar Refugees Back Home
1999-07-28.abstract: Kosovo refugees in United States respond eagerly to State Department offer to fly them home; some third of 10,513 refugees in US have already applied for flights (M)

1999-07-28.title: WORLD BRIEFING
1999-07-28.abstract: MIDDLE EAST    IRAN: PRESIDENT AFFIRMS STANCE -- President Mohammad Khatami, his reformist policies under attack by hard-liners after some of the worst unrest since the 1979 Islamic revolution, pledged to stand by his election promise to protect civil liberties. Mr. Khatami's speech, his first public appearance since pro-democracy student protests from July 8 to July 13, kicked off a three-day tour of the western province of Hamadan.   (Reuters)    IRAQ: NERVE GAS SET FOR DESTRUCTION -- The United Nations Security Council permitted an international arms team to proceed with the destruction of tiny specimens of the deadly nerve gas VX in Baghdad despite weeklong attempts by Russia to have the samples preserved. At issue was an attempt by Russia to have the chemical agents analyzed, a move suggesting suspicions by Moscow that the Special Commission, in charge of Iraqi disarmament, might have daubed VX on Iraqi missile warheads to fabricate evidence against Baghdad.   (Reuters)

1999-07-28.title: Serthar Journal; A 'Living Buddha' Plants an Academy
1999-07-28.abstract: Serthar, remote site in ethnic Tibetan region of China's Sichuan Province, has emerged in recent years as one of largest and most influential centers for Tibetan Buddhism; is brightest star among number of Tibetan Buddhist centers being built or rebuilt with private donations from Tibetans and reluctantly accepted by China; Khenpo Jikphun, master teacher known by follwers as 'living buddha' has attracted nearly 8,000 monks and nuns who live and study at center; map; photo (M)

1999-07-29.title: Vote Near on U.N. Job
1999-07-29.abstract: The Senate will probably vote next week on the long-delayed nomination of Richard C. Holbrooke as chief  delegate to the United Nations, a top Democrat said. Republicans have blocked the vote until the Administration addresses demands that have little to do with Mr. Holbrooke.  Article, page A5.

1999-07-29.title: A Sport Called Extreme, With Reason
1999-07-29.abstract: Article on canyoning notes that it has emerged as trendy 'extreme' sport in Europe and US, ranking somewhere between bungee jumping and whitewater rafting in its thrills and spills; it remains particularly popular in Switzerland, Austria and France as income-producing counterpart to traditional winter sports; diagram (M)

1999-07-29.title: For Gandhi (d.1948), a Long Due Apology
1999-07-29.abstract: Natal Law Society of South Africa, one hundred and five years after it tried to keep Mohandas K Gandhi from practicing law because he was 'not of European descent,' unconditionally apologizes for its actions; Gandhi, who had moved to South Africa to represent Indian-based trading company, was first nonwhite to apply for credentials from society; photo (M)

1999-07-29.title: Despite Maneuvers, China Seems Cautious on Taiwan
1999-07-29.abstract: Chinese news service reports that navy is conducting mobilization exercises along southern coast, across from Taiwan, but it appears to be relatively moderate operation that hints at little real military preparation; Chinese officials and Western diplomats, despite Beijing's insistence that it will use military force to block declaration of independence by Taiwan, say Beijing is shying away from conflict; photo (M)

1999-07-29.title: Toll Now 19 in Swiss Canyon In an Adventure Gone Awry
1999-07-29.abstract: Death toll rises to 19 in Swiss canyoning accident, as rescue workers search rapids for two missing tourists caught in flash flood in mountain stream near Interlaken, Switzerland; disaster has led to soul searching about adventure sports and in particular fad of canyoning; it is fueling questions about thin line between calculated risk and recklessness in most affluent sectors of society; Swiss authorities suspend all canyoning expeditions; map; photo (M)

1999-07-30.title: A Pillar of Opposition Who Keeps Belgrade Guessing
1999-07-30.abstract: Clinton Administration wants popular opposition leader Dragoslav Avramovic to become Prime Minister of Serbia and take leading role in effort to oust Yugoslav Pres Slobodan Milosevic, but he is 80 years old and has serious kidney troubles; photo (M)

1999-07-30.title: Phaidon Gizikis, '73 Greek Junta Officer, 82
1999-07-30.abstract: Phaidon Gizikis, Greek general who as President of Greece played key role in paving way for return of democracy after seven years of dictatorial rule, dies at age 82; photo (M)

1999-07-30.title: Followers of Chinese Sect Defend Its Spiritual Goals
1999-07-30.abstract: Followers of Falun Gong in Beijing express shock and sense of betrayal at Government's crackdown on their spiritual movement; crackdown appears to be classic clash of ancient Chinese culture with grim modern reality of Communist Party politics; Chinese police issue arrest warrant for Li Hongzhi, Falun Gong's founder, essentially meaningless gesture, since he lives in New York City and US does not have extradition treaty with China; photo (M)

1999-07-30.title: WORLD BRIEFING
1999-07-30.abstract: MIDDLE EAST    IRAQ: BOMBING DEATHS REPORTED -- Western air strikes killed 8 civilians and wounded 25 others in southern Iraq, Iraqi military officials said. British officials confirmed the strikes but said there was not a report yet on what had been hit. American and British warplanes clash regularly with Iraqi air defenses in their patrol of the northern and southern no-flight zones, established after the 1991 gulf war to help protect Kurd and Shiite Muslim dissidents.   (Reuters)    JORDAN: THE INCOGNITO KING -- King Abdullah, below left, showing glimpses of the common touch that made his late father, King Hussein, hugely popular, disguised himself with a white beard to investigate operations at a duty-free zone, palace officials confirmed. The 37-year-old King was accompanied by what appeared to be a television news crew who told businessmen that they were making a report on the zone, as Abdullah, who assumed the throne in February, listened attentively to complaints about a bloated bureaucracy.   (Reuters)

1999-07-30.title: Albright Tells Kosovars Not to Act Like Their Oppressors
1999-07-30.abstract: Sec of State Madeleine K Albright arrives in Kosovo on eve of Balkan summit meeting in Sarajevo, Bosnia; says if there is to be true victory in Kosovo, it cannot be victory of Albanians over Serbs, or NATO over Serbs, it must be victory of those who believe in rights of individual over those who do not; photo (M)

1999-07-31.title: World Leaders Join in Pledging Effort to Rebuild Balkans
1999-07-31.abstract: Thirty-nine world leaders, including Pres Clinton, meet in Sarajevo, Bosnia and Herzegovina, to inaugurate effort to rebuild southeastern Europe and its economy from ravages of decade of war; Clinton, the sole leader to pledge specific aid, announces package of $700 million in aid, trade programs and business incentives to generate investment in Balkans; package is in addition to $500 million in relief assistance earmarked specifically for Kosovo that United States pledged on July 28 in Brussels; leaders, more broadly, are seeking to avert future conflicts in Balkans through long-term economic reconstruction; photo of Clinton (M)

1999-07-31.title: WORLD BRIEFING
1999-07-31.abstract: Iran's Supreme leader, Ayatollah Ali Khamanei, blames 'hidden hands' of United States for pro-democracy protests this month, contending that Washington had tried to repeat 1953 coup that restored monarchy (S)

1999-07-31.title: NATO Holds 3 Albanians in Killing of 14 Serbs
1999-07-31.abstract: NATO police hold three Kosovar Albanians as suspects in murders of 14 Serbian farmers on July 23 in village of Gracko, and NATO sends weapons, ammunition and clothing to Britain for forensic evidence (S)

1999-07-31.title: Britain Nominates Its Defense Secretary to Be Head of NATO
1999-07-31.abstract: Prime Min Tony Blair nominates Defense Sec George Robertson, Scottish labor union veteran whose vigorous denunciations of Yugoslav military actions in Kosovo were almost as intense as NATO's bombing campaign, to be Britain's candidate for NATO Sec Gen; Robertson, 53, immediately becomes front-runner to succeed Javier Solana, who is soon to leave NATO job in Brussels to become European Union's coordinator for foreign and defense policy; Robertson photo (M)

1999-07-31.title: U.S. to Pay China for Embassy Bombing
1999-07-31.abstract: United States and China agree that US will pay $4.5 million in compensation for victims of NATO's bombing of Chinese Embassy in Yugoslavia; agreement removes one of biggest obstacles in relations between Washington and Beijing when several sensitive issues--Taiwan and trade, above all--remain on table as two nations prepare for meeting between Presidents Clinton and Jiang Zemin at Asia Pacific Economic Cooperation forum in New Zealand in September; photo of David Andrews, State Dept legal adviser who negotiated compensation accord with China (M)
